title: White Chocolate Cardamom Mousse (Not Dairy-Free)
subtitle: Patience and a freezer.
excerpt: White Chocolate Cardamom Mousse (Not Dairy-Free). A home-cook's recipe from Rebecca's collection, kept in the family rotation.
sourceNotes: From Rebecca's personal cookbook, a recipe she's made many times in her own kitchen.

content.text: White Chocolate Cardamom Mousse (Not Dairy-Free). A British home-cook's dish, the kind of recipe families adjust across generations. Adjustable for what you need.
A weeknight-friendly recipe: most of the work is the prep, and the cooking takes care of itself.

Break cardamom pods open and extract seeds (throw husk). Crush the seeds lightly then add to the milk and bay leaves in a small saucepan. Heat until it approaches boiling point then remove.Meanwhile, break chocolate into small pieces and melt in a bain marie.Whip the cream so it forms soft peaks. Beat the egg whites to stiff peaks.When the chocolate is completely melted remove from heat and combine with the warm milk, which should be sieved first to remove the bay leaves and seeds. Once it is a velvety texture  scoop the mixture on to the stiffly beaten egg whites and fold gently with a metal spoon. Now add the whipped cream.Scrape mixture into a serving bowl or espresso cups and refrigerate for 3-4 hours until set (I think if you've used an electric whisk your mousse will probably set firmer than mine did! What's that about a bad workman blaming his tools..hmm) Dust with cocoa powder or do a dark chocolate sauce with it as I did -

Melt the chocolate and butter in a bain marie. Stir in the sugar and syrup until dissolved (I put less in than is recommended above as the mousse is so sweet I think it needs to be a bit more bitter to balance it, then pour in the milk and continue to cook stirring often until the sauce thickens.

attrs.intro: A few of the more common issues, and what tends to fix them.

items.fix: Churn to a soft-serve consistency before freezing. Pre-chill the bowl overnight if using a bowl-style ice cream maker.
items.cause: Mixture had too much water, or didn't churn long enough.

items.fix: Take out 15 minutes before serving to soften. Next time, scale up sugar by 10 to 20 grams per 500 ml of mixture.
items.cause: Sugar level too low, sugar prevents freezing solid.
items.symptom: Texture is too hard from the freezer

items.fix: Temper, pour a ladle of hot milk into the yolks first, whisking, then return to the pan over low heat. Don't let it boil.
items.cause: Heat too high, or eggs went in cold and fast.

items.fix: Whip cream to soft peaks (it flops slightly when the whisk is lifted). Fold in with a metal spoon, cutting through the centre and lifting from the base.
items.cause: Cream wasn't whipped enough, or it was folded in too vigorously.

attrs.heading: If something goes wrong

content.text: Boozy version: stir in 2 tablespoons of dark rum, brandy, or liqueur once the mix is churned, the alcohol stops it freezing hard.
Chocolate ripple: drizzle warm chocolate sauce through the churned mix before freezing, and don't over-stir.
Made days in advance and lives in the freezer.
A British home cook's dish. It sits among the recipes families remember without thinking about, the kind that gets adjusted across generations and never quite the same twice.
